paragraphs: The Bush lemon is the naturalised rough lemon (Citrus jambhiri), a vigorous citrus hybrid of mandarin and citron that escaped cultivation and grows wild across subtropical Australia. Hardy, drought resistant and cold tolerant, it adapted to tough conditions and now grows feral, which is how it earned the bush lemon name. It forms a large, often thorny tree.

Its fruit has a thick, rough, knobbly rind and is seedy, making it mostly rind and less suited to eating than selected lemons like Eureka or Lisbon. Its main historical value is as a rootstock, valued for vigour and drought tolerance, onto which finer lemon and citrus cultivars are budded. The fruit is still used at home for juice and zest.

claims.text: Bush lemon is the rough lemon, Citrus jambhiri, a cross of mandarin orange and citron
Naturalised and growing wild (feral) across subtropical Australia
Hardy, drought resistant and cold-hardy, forming a large tree
Fruit is mostly rough rind and seedy, less edible than selected lemons
Mainly used as a citrus rootstock, valued for drought tolerance

sources.name: Wikipedia: Rough lemon

paragraphs: Eureka originated in Los Angeles, California, selected from seedlings grown from Italian seed and introduced around 1877 as Garey's Eureka. Along with Lisbon it became one of the world's principal lemon varieties.

The tree is largely thornless and strongly everbearing, producing fruit at the ends of long branches, which makes it a popular choice for home gardens. The acidic fruit has a fairly rough, prominently ridged rind and only a small nipple.

claims.text: Originated in Los Angeles, California, from seedlings of Italian seed and introduced around 1877 as Garey's Eureka
Along with Lisbon, became one of the world's principal lemon varieties
Tree is largely thornless and strongly everbearing, fruiting at the ends of long branches
Acidic fruit with a fairly rough, prominently ridged rind and a small nipple

sources.name: Givaudan Citrus Variety Collection (UC Riverside): Frost nucellar Eureka lemon

paragraphs: Eureka Seedless is a seedless selection of the Eureka lemon, the cultivar that originated in California around 1858 from seedlings raised in Los Angeles from fruit of Sicilian origin. The parent Eureka is a thornless, highly productive tree that fruits across much of the year, with the main crop running from late winter into early summer.

The defining feature of this selection is fruit that is virtually free of seeds, developed to meet demand for a clean, easy to use lemon. The juice is sharp and acidic in true Eureka style, and the fruit is oblong with a thin, bright yellow rind. Several seedless Eureka lines are grown in Australia, all chosen for that near seedless quality.

claims.text: Eureka originated in California around 1858 from seedlings raised in Los Angeles from fruit of Sicilian origin.
The parent Eureka is thornless and highly productive, fruiting much of the year with the main crop from late winter to early summer.
This selection produces fruit that is virtually seedless.
The fruit is oblong with a thin bright yellow rind and sharp, acidic juice.

paragraphs: Eureka Variegated, also sold as Variegated Pink, is an ornamental sport of the Eureka lemon that originated in California. It is grown above all for its variegated foliage, green leaves marbled with cream, and for young fruit carrying distinct green and cream stripes.

When the fruit ripens the striping fades to a yellow rind marked with pink oil glands, and inside the flesh is tinged pink rather than the usual pale lemon. The juice stays sharply acidic with few seeds, true to Eureka, so the tree offers both a usable crop and year round decorative appeal.

claims.text: It is an ornamental, pink-fleshed sport of the Eureka lemon that originated in California.
The foliage is variegated green and cream, and young fruit is striped green and cream.
On ripening the stripes fade to a yellow rind with pink oil glands, and the flesh is tinged pink.
The juice is sharply acidic with few seeds, like standard Eureka.

paragraphs: Eureka Variegated Pink is the variegated, pink fleshed sport of the Eureka lemon, which arose in California early in the twentieth century. Its standout trait is ornamental: leaves splashed and edged with cream, and young fruit boldly striped in green and cream.

As the fruit ripens the green stripes fade and the rind turns yellow, often dotted with pink oil glands, while the flesh matures to a light pink. The fruit stays low in seeds and very acidic in classic Eureka style, making this a cultivar valued as much for its decorative foliage and striped fruit as for the lemons themselves.

claims.text: It is a variegated, pink-fleshed sport of the Eureka lemon that arose in California.
The foliage is variegated with cream, and young fruit is striped green and cream.
As fruit ripens the stripes fade to a yellow rind marked with pink oil glands, and the flesh is light pink.

paragraphs: Fino is a leading Spanish commercial lemon, the most widely grown cultivar in Spain, probably arising as a seedling of a common Murcia-region lemon. It is an early to mid-season (mainly winter) lemon. The tree is exceptionally vigorous, large, very thorny and highly productive, with dense foliage.

Compared with the other major Spanish lemon, Berna (Verna), Fino fruit is paler, with a smoother and thinner rind, and is higher in juice and acid content, though more seedy. The fruit is spherical to oval with a small, sharp-pointed nipple. Its high juice yield and acidity make it a favourite of processors.

claims.text: Fino probably originated as a seedling of a common Murcia-region lemon and is a major Spanish cultivar
Crop comes mainly in winter (early to mid season)
Tree is exceptionally vigorous, large, very thorny and highly productive
Fruit is paler with smoother, thinner rind and higher juice and acid content than Berna, but more seedy
Fruit is spherical to oval with a small, sharp-pointed nipple

paragraphs: Lemon Heaven is a branded seedless selection of the classic Eureka lemon (the same product is often listed as Heaven Seedless Eureka), sold in Australia under plant breeder rights through PlantNet, which credits its development to the citrus breeding program of South Africa's Agricultural Research Council. Its claim to fame is fruit that is all lemon and no seeds, so juice goes straight in without straining or picking out pips.

In the garden it behaves like a vigorous Eureka: a self fertile tree of around 4 metres that crops prolifically in large bunches of medium to large, thick skinned, juicy and sour fruit. Harvest begins around March in warmer districts and April or May in cooler ones and can continue for several months, and PlantNet also offers the variety on dwarf rootstock for pots.

claims.text: A seedless selection of the Eureka lemon, PBR protected and bred by the citrus breeding program of the Agricultural Research Council of South Africa
Also marketed as Heaven Seedless Eureka; exclusive to the PlantNet brand
Fruit is seedless, so juice needs no straining or seed picking
Prolific bearer producing large bunches of medium to large, thick skinned, juicy sour fruit
Harvest from about March in warmer areas and April or May in cooler regions, continuing for several months
Vigorous, self fertile tree growing to roughly 4 metres, with a dwarf rootstock version available for containers

paragraphs: Lemon Heaven is a branded seedless selection of the classic Eureka lemon, sold in Australia under plant breeder rights through PlantNet, which credits its development to the citrus breeding program of South Africa's Agricultural Research Council. The selling point is fruit that is all lemon and no seeds, so juice can be squeezed straight in without straining or picking out pips.

Otherwise it behaves like a vigorous Eureka: a self fertile tree of around 4 metres that crops prolifically in large bunches, with medium to large, thick skinned, juicy and sour fruit. Harvest begins around March in warmer districts and April or May in cooler ones and can stretch on for several months, and PlantNet also offers the same variety on dwarf rootstock for pots.

paragraphs: Lemonade is not a true lemon but a sweet, low-acid hybrid widely believed to be a cross of lemon and mandarin, discovered as a chance seedling in New Zealand in the early 1980s and now grown across Australia. Its exact parentage remains uncertain. The low acidity lets the natural sugars come through, giving a mild, sweet, faintly tangy flavour quite unlike a sour lemon.

Unlike a cooking lemon, ripe Lemonade fruit can be peeled and eaten fresh straight off the tree, segmenting easily thanks to its mandarin heritage. The fruit has a glossy yellow rind and juicy yellow flesh, sweetens the longer it hangs on the tree, and is also juiced for a naturally sweet drink. The tree is heavy bearing and often thorny.

claims.text: Sweet, low-acid hybrid generally regarded as a lemon by mandarin cross
Discovered as a chance seedling in New Zealand in the early 1980s
Low acidity gives a sweet, mild flavour rather than sour
Can be peeled and eaten fresh off the tree, segmenting like a mandarin
Tree is heavy bearing and often thorny

paragraphs: Lemonicious is an Australian retail selection of the Meyer lemon, the naturally sweet lemon and mandarin orange hybrid, picked out for an unusually small habit. It is marketed as a super dwarf or patio lemon, topping out at roughly 1.5 to 2 metres, which makes it one of the most compact lemon options sold in Australia and a natural fit for pots on balconies, courtyards and decks.

The medium sized, thin skinned fruit carries typical Meyer character: noticeably less acidic and a touch sweeter than a true lemon such as Eureka or Lisbon. Retailers describe fragrant white flowers over a long season and a hardy constitution for a citrus, with listings claiming tolerance of frost, humidity and coastal positions.

claims.text: Lemonicious is sold as a dwarf selection of the Meyer lemon, a lemon and mandarin orange hybrid
Compact habit of roughly 1.5 to 2 metres, marketed for pots, patios and courtyards
Fruit is less acidic and sweeter than a true lemon, in the Meyer style
Medium sized, thin skinned fruit
Fragrant white flowers over a long season
Hardy for a citrus, with claimed tolerance of frost, humidity and coastal conditions

paragraphs: Lisbon is a lemon of Portuguese origin, believed to be a Gallego seedling selection, and was introduced to Australia early in the 1800s. It became one of the leading commercial lemons in both Australia and the United States.

The tree is vigorous, large, thorny and densely foliated, and is notably resistant to frost, heat, wind and neglect, more so than Eureka. Because much of the fruit is borne within the canopy it is well protected, and the oblong, very acidic fruit carries a prominent nipple.

claims.text: Lemon of Portuguese origin, believed to be a Gallego seedling selection, introduced to Australia early in the 1800s
Became a leading commercial lemon in Australia and the United States
Tree is vigorous, large, thorny and densely foliated, and notably resistant to frost, heat, wind and neglect, more so than Eureka
Much of the fruit is borne within the canopy, providing protection

paragraphs: Sold in Australia as Lots A Lemons, this is a dwarf form of the Meyer lemon, the small growing citrus thought to be a cross between a lemon and an orange. The compact bush stays around 1.5 metres tall and wide yet bears full sized, very juicy fruit, which is the whole point of the variety: a tub sized tree producing normal Meyer lemons.

The fruit has classic Meyer character, sweeter and less acidic than a true lemon, and the bush carries fragrant creamy white blossom. Yates specifically recommends growing it in a pot to guarantee drainage, and it inherits Meyer's cold tolerance, making it one of the more reliable lemon choices for cooler regions and frost prone areas.

claims.text: Lots A Lemons is a dwarf form of the Meyer lemon
Meyer is a small grower thought to be a cross between a lemon and an orange
Grows to about 1.5 metres tall and wide but bears full sized, very juicy fruit
Fruit is sweeter and less acidic than a true lemon

paragraphs: The Meyer lemon is native to China and was introduced to the United States in 1908 by USDA plant explorer Frank Meyer. It is not a true lemon but a hybrid, generally described as a cross of citron with a mandarin/pomelo type.

Its fruit is sweeter and less acidic than standard Eureka or Lisbon lemons, with thin, deep-yellow to faintly orange skin and a subtle floral, almost orange-like character. A virus-free selection was released by the University of California in 1975 as the Improved Meyer lemon.

claims.text: Native to China and introduced to the United States in 1908 by USDA plant explorer Frank Meyer
Not a true lemon but a hybrid, generally a cross of citron with a mandarin/pomelo type
Sweeter and less acidic than Eureka or Lisbon lemons, with a subtle orange-like character
Thin, deep-yellow to faintly orange skin
A virus-free selection was released by the University of California in 1975 as the Improved Meyer lemon

sources.name: Wikipedia: Meyer lemon

paragraphs: Myer is the common Australian spelling of the Meyer lemon, named after Frank N. Meyer, a USDA plant explorer who collected it in China and introduced it to the United States in 1908. It is not a true lemon but a hybrid, a cross between a citron and a mandarin/pomelo hybrid, which gives it its mild character.

The fruit is rounder than a true lemon, with thin skin that ripens to deep yellow with a slight orange tint. The flesh is juicy, noticeably sweeter and far less acidic than a Eureka or Lisbon, almost tangerine-like. A virus-free clone, the Improved Meyer lemon, is the form most widely grown today, having replaced earlier stock that carried tristeza virus.

claims.text: Myer is the Australian spelling of the Meyer lemon, named after USDA plant explorer Frank N. Meyer.
It was collected in China and introduced to the United States in 1908 by Frank N. Meyer.
It is not a true lemon but a cross between a citron and a mandarin/pomelo hybrid.
The fruit is rounder than a true lemon, deep yellow with an orange tint, sweeter and less acidic.

paragraphs: Pinky is the Australian trade name for the variegated pink fleshed Eureka lemon, a chance sport found on an ordinary Eureka tree in Burbank, California, and selected in 1931. It is grown as much for looks as for fruit: the foliage is splashed green and cream, young fruit are striped green and yellow, and the stripes fade as the lemons ripen. Inside, the flesh matures to a light rosy pink, the trait behind overseas names like Pink Lemonade.

The fruit itself is still a proper acid lemon, sharp and juicy with very few seeds, so it works anywhere a standard Eureka would. Australian sellers describe a tree of around 3 to 4 metres that also suits large containers, where the variegated leaves and striped fruit make it a striking feature plant.

claims.text: Originated as a spontaneous sport on a Eureka lemon in Burbank, California, selected in 1931
Sold in Australia under the trade name Pinky; the same variety is marketed overseas as Pink Lemonade
Variegated green and cream foliage and green striped fruit, with the striping fading as fruit ripens
Flesh develops a light pink colour at maturity
Sharp, acidic, juicy fruit
Low seeded fruit
Tree grows to around 3 to 4 metres and suits pots and containers

paragraphs: Prior Lisbon is one of the many named selections of the Lisbon lemon, a vigorous true lemon believed to be a Gallego seedling of Portuguese origin. Lisbon reached Australia as early as 1824 before being carried on to California, and numerous distinct Lisbon clones, Prior among them, have since been propagated.

Lisbon trees grow into large, thorny, upright but spreading specimens, so the fruit often sits within the canopy. The fruit is medium and oblong with a prominent nipple, a slightly textured yellow rind, and pale greenish yellow flesh that is low in seeds and very acidic. Lisbon is hardier than Eureka, tolerating both heat and cold, with the main crop in winter and early spring.

claims.text: Lisbon is believed to be a Gallego seedling of Portuguese origin and reached Australia as early as 1824 before going on to California.
Prior Lisbon is one of many named selections of the Lisbon lemon.
Lisbon trees are large, thorny and upright but spreading; fruit is medium, oblong, with a prominent nipple, textured yellow rind and pale greenish yellow, low-seeded, very acidic flesh.
Lisbon is hardier than Eureka, tolerant of heat and cold, with the main crop in winter and early spring.

paragraphs: Seedless Eureka is a selection of the classic Eureka lemon that arose as a limb sport (bud mutation) and is distinguished from ordinary Eureka by its consistently seedless fruit. In other respects it closely resembles its parent, carrying the same bright, tart, high-acid lemon flavour collectors expect from a Eureka.

The fruit is medium to large with smooth, thin yellow skin that zests and slices cleanly, and like the Eureka the tree tends to be near everbearing, cropping over much of the year rather than in one short flush. The seedlessness makes it convenient for juicing and cooking without picking out pips.

claims.text: Seedless Eureka is a selection of Eureka that arose as a limb sport (bud mutation)
Distinguished from ordinary Eureka by consistently seedless fruit, otherwise similar
Retains the classic tart, high-acid Eureka lemon flavour
Eureka type tends to be near everbearing, cropping over much of the year

paragraphs: The Variegated Eureka, also called the variegated pink lemon or pink-fleshed Eureka, is an ornamental sport that appeared on an ordinary Eureka lemon tree in Burbank, California, in 1931. It is prized as much for its looks as its fruit, with green-and-cream variegated foliage and immature fruit that is striped green and yellow before ripening to yellow.

Inside, the flesh is a soft pink (similar in look to a pink grapefruit), though the juice runs clear rather than pink. The flavour is a true acidic lemon, useful in the kitchen wherever a standard Eureka would be, so the variety offers the novelty of pink flesh and striped, variegated growth without losing classic lemon tartness.

claims.text: Variegated Eureka is the variegated pink lemon, a sport on a Eureka tree in Burbank, California, in 1931
Has green-and-cream variegated foliage and green-striped rind before ripening
Flesh is pink while the juice is clear rather than pink
Flavour is a true acidic lemon, used like a standard Eureka

paragraphs: Verna (also spelt Berna) is a major Spanish lemon of unknown origin, historically the dominant lemon of the Spanish Levant and still the second most grown Spanish cultivar after Fino. It is a distinctly late-season variety, ripening from late winter into summer and holding well on the tree, which extends the supply beyond earlier lemons.

The fruit is medium sized, oval to broad-elliptical, with a short neck, a well developed nipple and bright yellow rind at maturity. The medium-thick rind is somewhat pebbled and rough, and seed content is variable but usually few to none. It is grown primarily as a fresh-market table lemon.

Late-season variety ripening from late winter into summer and holding well

paragraphs: Villa Franca is an old lemon of Sicilian origin, introduced to Florida around 1875 and soon after brought to California. The tree resembles Lisbon but grows more open and less upright, with few or no persistent thorns and sparser foliage.

Its fruit is essentially indistinguishable from Eureka, oval and low in seeds with juicy, intensely lemony flesh, yet the cropping pattern follows Lisbon, falling mainly in winter. This combination of Eureka-like fruit on a Lisbon-like seasonal habit is what sets Villa Franca apart.

claims.text: Villa Franca is of Sicilian origin, introduced to Florida around 1875 and soon after to California.
The tree resembles Lisbon but is more open and less upright, with few or no persistent thorns and sparser foliage.
The fruit is indistinguishable from Eureka, oval and low-seeded with juicy, intensely lemony flesh.
The cropping pattern follows Lisbon, with fruit mainly in winter.

paragraphs: Yuzu (Citrus junos) is a distinct cold-hardy citrus, not a true lemon. It is an F1 hybrid of a mandarin and the ichang papeda, originating in central China and long cultivated in Japan and Korea. Its papeda ancestry makes it unusually frost tolerant, growing where more sensitive citrus would fail (winters down to around minus 7 degrees Celsius). The fruit resembles a small, bumpy yellow to green mandarin with uneven skin.

Yuzu is prized for its intensely aromatic rind and tart, seedy juice rather than for fresh eating, which it is rarely used for. The zest and juice carry a complex fragrance blending lemon, mandarin and grapefruit with herbal notes, and feature heavily in Japanese and Korean cooking, including the citrus sauce ponzu and the chilli paste yuzu kosho.

claims.text: Yuzu is Citrus junos, an F1 hybrid of a mandarin and the ichang papeda
Originated in central China and is cultivated in Japan and Korea
One of the most cold-hardy citrus, tolerating winters to about minus 7 degrees Celsius
Valued for aromatic rind and tart juice, rarely eaten fresh
Used in ponzu and yuzu kosho in Japanese and Korean cooking

paragraphs: Yuzu Japanese is the yuzu, Citrus junos, a citrus of Chinese origin that is a hybrid of mandarin orange and the Ichang papeda. That papeda parentage makes it one of the hardiest of all citrus, able to grow and fruit where frost would kill most lemons.

The fruit resembles a small, uneven, bumpy lemon, ripening from green to yellow, with a thick rind packed with aromatic oils. It is rarely eaten fresh, prized instead for its fragrant zest and tart juice, which are central to Japanese and Korean cooking and to ponzu sauce. The flavour is tart and aromatic, recalling grapefruit with mandarin overtones.

claims.text: Yuzu (Citrus junos) is of Chinese origin and a hybrid of mandarin orange and the Ichang papeda.
Its papeda ancestry makes it one of the hardiest citrus, tolerating frost that kills most lemons.
The fruit has a bumpy, uneven rind and ripens from green to yellow.
It is rarely eaten fresh; the aromatic zest and tart juice are used in Japanese and Korean cooking and in ponzu.
The flavour is tart and fragrant, resembling grapefruit with mandarin overtones.

sources.name: UC Riverside Citrus Variety Collection: Yuzu ichandrin

paragraphs: Yuzu Japanese Citron refers to yuzu, Citrus junos, a hybrid of mandarin and the Ichang papeda that originated in central China. Despite the citron in this nursery name, it is a papeda cross, and that ancestry gives it unusual cold tolerance for a citrus, fruiting where frost would harm most lemons.

Its bumpy, clementine-like fruit turns yellow or green with ripeness and carries a heavily aromatic rind. Seldom eaten as fresh fruit, yuzu is valued for the perfumed zest and sharp juice used much like lemon across Japanese and Korean cuisine, including ponzu. The taste is tart and aromatic, close to grapefruit with notes of mandarin.

claims.text: Yuzu (Citrus junos) originated in central China and is a hybrid of mandarin and the Ichang papeda, not a true citron.
Its papeda ancestry gives it unusual cold tolerance for a citrus.
The fruit is bumpy and clementine-like, turning yellow or green with ripeness, with a heavily aromatic rind.
It is seldom eaten fresh; the zest and juice are used in Japanese and Korean cuisine, including ponzu.
The taste is tart and aromatic, close to grapefruit with mandarin notes.
